verbatim_text: So what are some examples of these algorithms? They can either be a piece of code, a program, or it can also be the weights of a network with a feedback loop. Now the defining property in both these cases is that the operations can be variable and they can be scalable, depending on that particular input. Now as these operations get more and more expressive, these algorithms can actually solve open-ended problems. I'll give you an example here. So let's say there is some research problem. You want to prove some open conjecture and partial differential equations. You feed that as input of the algorithm. It's unclear what this trace of operation should look like, but a good algorithm would produce something like this. It would first retrieve some prior work relevant to this research problem, build some simulations, run it, collect data, fit that data. Maybe even come up with a proof, try to verify that proof using some tool like Lean, and then send that as sort of feedback to the user. Now if we squint at it, at a high level, this trace of operations looks like a form of intelligent test time search. And that's a good example to keep in mind throughout this talk. So a good algorithm is one that's doing some intelligent test time search.
